What I'm talking about here is what happens after you close the sale. In other words, how do you take someone that buys from you once and probably it's a relatively small transaction because I actually, if you really are studying the straight line and you all are, right? You understand that, and I'm going to go through this in a second because I did it on today's motivation that the basis of the straight line is like, you know, give me one shot. Like I understand that we don't know each other. We don't have a track record of working together. So just give me one chance to show you my stuff. We can work smaller this time and then work bigger and better down the road. Now in some industries, that's not possible, right? But in most industries, it is in almost some way in any industry, it's almost always possible to bring someone into the fold, so to speak, to have them become a customer and starting them off with a smaller commitment upfront and then you're upselling them and reselling them as the relationship builds and grows and they trust you more, they get to experience the benefits of your product and they see that what you actually said not only was real but even exceeded their expectations, hopefully, you've moved their pain now and now instead of making the small sale, you can make a much larger sale. For example, if you're in the business, let's say you work in a sales of like an audio video group, right? You might have come and, you know, came to someone's house, they were skeptical, they had four or five bids, you're trying to sell them an AV system and maybe you installed an AV system that was kind of a quick fix, you may even have a TV set, repair something and charge $50, seriously. And then all of a sudden though, you did a great job, you said, I'm going to go, I want to give away the keys here. Yeah, I was going to say what you do to create that sort of customer file. Let's see, you did a few things afterwards, which I'll teach you in this lesson here, and you did these certain things that made them just really respect you and like you and want to only use you in that $150 sale and becoming the $40,000 full review of their whole house. That happens all the time. Another example is like trained plumbing company in Australia many, many years ago. And what we did to radically increase their sales, in fact, it increased their sales so much, it almost blew them up by the way, they had to call me back in to reorganize the operations is by taking their calls and because plumbers mostly get emergency calls. That's basically like my pipe broke, I'm fix my pipe, right? And they would have, you know, they were having plumbers go out and fix the pipes and they'd get a $80 sale and leave a business card and hope they got a new customer out of their real customer, right? Instead, I changed the whole premise where the plumbers would show up for the emergency thing with a clipboard and they say, oh yeah, of course, do that in fact, why me? Which like to do a quick once over into a whole plumbing health check of your house, you know, free of charge, they're like, oh yeah, great, you know, and they do the whole thing and there you go. And then they would, and the problem was they would find so many things wrong to clean a home that it would scoop their schedule. They couldn't even have plumbers ready for emergency calls. So the guy would go there for an hour job, we'll turn it to a three day job. So we had to do is create two teams of plumbers, plumbers that went out on the emergency calls or really the sales force that could do a quick fix and were proficient at selling with the straight line. And then the bigger jobs that came from that became scheduled jobs, two weeks down the road, you send the actual team of plumbers that really can sell, but can do the operations. You understand some of the other is there's all these things that you could do to get a small customer and turn customer. And that's what this training is all about.
